revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''棟札'''（むなふだ・むねふだ）は、[[建物]]の[[棟上]]や[[改築]]・修繕など際、その年月日や建築主・大工の名・工事の...'
梁上銘、材質、解体修理時の発見のことなど
revision.text:
'''棟札'''（むなふだ、むねふだ）は、寺社・民家など建物の建築・修築の記録・記念として、棟木・梁など建物内部の高所に取り付けた札である。典型的には、木の札または銅の板に記して釘で打ち付ける。中には建物の部材に直接記されることもあり、これを梁上銘と呼ぶこともあるが、趣旨は同じである<ref>沼田頼輔「棟札の沿革」、『考古学雑誌』第8巻第7号（通編222号）、1918年3月。</ref>。
書かれる内容は築造・修理の目的を知るした意趣文やその年月日や建築主・大工の名・工事の目的など建築記録を記すが、関連して他の事に及ぶものもある。簡潔なものもあれば、詳細に記されたもの、絵柄が記されたものなど多種多様である。棟札は普通の利用者には見えない位置に取り付けられるため、年月か経つとしばしば存在が忘れられてしまう。そうした棟札が解体・修理の際に発見されることがある。
棟札に記されていることは誤記などもあるが、その建物をはじめその地域の歴史や文化に関する重要な歴史的史料となり、[[文化財]]に指定されているものも多い。最古の棟札は、岩手県[[中尊寺]]の[[保安 (日本)|保安]]3年（1122年）銘をもつものであるという。
==脚注== <references />
{{DEFAULTSORT:むなふた}} [[category:寺院建築]] {{history-stub}}

revision.comment: r2.7.1) (ロボットによる 追加: [[zh:棟札]]
revision.text:
'''棟札'''（むなふだ、むねふだ）は、寺社・民家など建物の建築・修築の記録・記念として、棟木・梁など建物内部の高所に取り付けた札である。典型的には、木の札または銅の板に記して釘で打ち付ける。中には建物の部材に直接記されることもあり、これを梁上銘と呼ぶこともあるが、趣旨は同じである<ref>沼田頼輔「棟札の沿革」、『考古学雑誌』第8巻第7号（通編222号）、1918年3月。</ref>。
書かれる内容は築造・修理の目的を記した意趣文やその年月日や建築主・大工の名・工事の目的など建築記録だが、関連して他の事に及ぶものもある。簡潔なものもあれば、詳細に記されたもの、絵柄が記されたものなど多種多様である。棟札は普通の利用者には見えない位置に取り付けられるため、年月か経つとしばしば存在が忘れられてしまう。そうした棟札が解体・修理の際に発見されることがある。
棟札に記されていることは誤記などもあるが、その建物をはじめその地域の歴史や文化に関する重要な歴史的史料となり、[[文化財]]に指定されているものも多い。最古の棟札は、岩手県[[中尊寺]]の[[保安 (日本)|保安]]3年（1122年）銘をもつものであるという。
==文献に現れた棟札== 建治元年の日蓮遺文に 「一、棟札の事承り候。書き候ひて此の伯耆公に進らせ候。此の経文は須達長者祇園精舎を造りき。然るに何なる因縁にやよりけん、 須達長者七度まで火災にあひ候時、長者此の由を仏に問ひ奉る…」（[http://homepage3.nifty.com/y-maki/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf 上野殿御書]） との表記がある。
==脚注== {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:むなふた}} [[category:寺院建築]] [[Category:歴史資料]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[zh:棟札]]

棟札に記されていることは誤記などもあるが、その建物をはじめその地域の歴史や文化に関する重要な歴史的史料となり、[[文化財]]に指定されているものも多い。最古の棟札は、岩手県[[中尊寺]]の[[保安 (日本)|保安]]3年（1122年）銘をもつものであるという。

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=棟札 |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=[[長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|久我屋敷]]の棟札、明治12年の文字が見える。 <!-- 中文 --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- 日文 --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munahuda、Munehuda }}
'''棟札'''（むなふだ、むねふだ）は、[[寺社]]・[[住宅|民家]]など建物の建築・修築の記録・記念として、棟木・[[梁 (建築)|梁]]など建物内部の高所に取り付けた[[札]]である。
== 概説 == 典型的には、木の札または銅の板に記して釘で打ち付ける。中には建物の部材に直接記されることもあり、これを梁上銘と呼ぶこともあるが、趣旨は同じである<ref>沼田頼輔「棟札の沿革」、『考古学雑誌』第8巻第7号（通編222号）、1918年3月。</ref>。
書かれる内容は築造・修理の目的を記した意趣文やその年月日や建築主・大工の名・工事の目的など建築記録だが、関連して他の事に及ぶものもある。簡潔なものもあれば、詳細に記されたもの、絵柄が記されたものなど多種多様である。棟札は普通の利用者には見えない位置に取り付けられるため、年月か経つとしばしば存在が忘れられてしまう。そうした棟札が解体・修理の際に発見されることがある。
棟札に記されていることは誤記などもあるが、その建物をはじめその地域の歴史や文化に関する重要な歴史的[[史料]]となり、[[文化財]]に指定されているものも多い。最古の棟札は、岩手県[[中尊寺]]の[[保安 (日本)|保安]]3年（1122年）銘をもつものであるという。
==文献に現れた棟札== 建治元年の日蓮遺文に 「一、棟札の事承り候。書き候ひて此の伯耆公に進らせ候。此の経文は須達長者祇園精舎を造りき。然るに何なる因縁にやよりけん、 須達長者七度まで火災にあひ候時、長者此の由を仏に問ひ奉る…」（[http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf 上野殿御書]） との表記がある。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}参考文献
秋山敬『棟札の基礎的研究』(2010年２月、岩田書院)
{{日本の建築}} {{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:むなふた}} [[Category:寺院建築]] [[Category:歴史資料]] [[Category:東アジアの伝統建築意匠]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|temples|ja|寺社}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.
{{Japanese architecture}} {{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Historical records}}

Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

[[Category:Munafuda]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Munafuda-qq]] [[Category:Temple architecture]] [[Category:Munafuda]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Munafuda-qq]] [[Category:Historical records]] [[Category:Munafuda]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Munafuda-qq]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian architectural elements]] [[Category:Munafuda]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Munafuda-qq]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Munafuda]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Munafuda]][[Category:qq]] [[zh:%E4%B8%8A%E6%A8%91%E8%A8%98%E7%89%8C]] [[ja:棟札]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Munafuda]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Munafuda]][[Category:11]]

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (qq22).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (qq22).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (qq22).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.
{{Japanese architecture}} {{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Historical records}}

However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

revision.text:
{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|12=draft}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879). <!-- Chinese --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- Japanese --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=draft}} and {{ill|houses|ja|住宅|12=draft}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=draft}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=draft}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|ja|中尊寺|12=draft}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

revision.text:
{{Infobox East Asian name |title=棟札 |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=[[長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|久我屋敷]]の棟札、明治12年の文字が見える。 <!-- 中文 --> |chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌 <!-- 日文 --> |japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munahuda、Munehuda }}
'''棟札'''（むなふだ、むねふだ）は、[[寺社]]・[[住宅|民家]]など建物の建築・修築の記録・記念として、棟木・[[梁 (建築)|梁]]など建物内部の高所に取り付けた[[札]]である。
== 概説 == 典型的には、木の札または銅の板に記して釘で打ち付ける。中には建物の部材に直接記されることもあり、これを梁上銘と呼ぶこともあるが、趣旨は同じである<ref>沼田頼輔「棟札の沿革」、『考古学雑誌』第8巻第7号（通編222号）、1918年3月。</ref>。
書かれる内容は築造・修理の目的を記した意趣文やその年月日や建築主・大工の名・工事の目的など建築記録だが、関連して他の事に及ぶものもある。簡潔なものもあれば、詳細に記されたもの、絵柄が記されたものなど多種多様である。棟札は普通の利用者には見えない位置に取り付けられるため、年月が経つとしばしば存在が忘れられてしまう。そうした棟札が解体・修理の際に発見されることがある。
棟札に記されていることは誤記などもあるが、その建物をはじめその地域の歴史や文化に関する重要な歴史的[[史料]]となり、[[文化財]]に指定されているものも多い。最古の棟札は、岩手県[[中尊寺]]の[[保安 (日本)|保安]]3年（1122年）銘をもつものであるという。
==文献に現れた棟札== 建治元年の日蓮遺文に 「一、棟札の事承り候。書き候ひて此の伯耆公に進らせ候。此の経文は須達長者祇園精舎を造りき。然るに何なる因縁にやよりけん、 須達長者七度まで火災にあひ候時、長者此の由を仏に問ひ奉る…」（[http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf 上野殿御書]） との表記がある。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}参考文献
秋山敬『棟札の基礎的研究』(2010年２月、岩田書院)
{{日本の建築}} {{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{歴史資料}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:むなふた}} [[Category:寺院建築]] [[Category:歴史資料]] [[Category:東アジアの伝統建築意匠]]

'''棟札'''（むなふだ、むねふだ）は、[[寺社]]・[[住宅|民家]]など建物の建築・修築の記録・記念として、棟木・[[梁 (建築)|梁]]など建物内部の高所に取り付けた[[札]]である。
== 概説 == 典型的には、木の札または銅の板に記して釘で打ち付ける。中には建物の部材に直接記されることもあり、これを梁上銘と呼ぶこともあるが、趣旨は同じである<ref>沼田頼輔「棟札の沿革」、『考古学雑誌』第8巻第7号（通編222号）、1918年3月。</ref>。
書かれる内容は築造・修理の目的を記した意趣文やその年月日や建築主・大工の名・工事の目的など建築記録だが、関連して他の事に及ぶものもある。簡潔なものもあれば、詳細に記されたもの、絵柄が記されたものなど多種多様である。棟札は普通の利用者には見えない位置に取り付けられるため、年月が経つとしばしば存在が忘れられてしまう。そうした棟札が解体・修理の際に発見されることがある。
棟札に記されていることは誤記などもあるが、その建物をはじめその地域の歴史や文化に関する重要な歴史的[[史料]]となり、[[文化財]]に指定されているものも多い。最古の棟札は、岩手県[[中尊寺]]の[[保安 (日本)|保安]]3年（1122年）銘をもつものであるという。
==文献に現れた棟札== 建治元年の日蓮遺文に 「一、棟札の事承り候。書き候ひて此の伯耆公に進らせ候。此の経文は須達長者祇園精舎を造りき。然るに何なる因縁にやよりけん、 須達長者七度まで火災にあひ候時、長者此の由を仏に問ひ奉る…」（[http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf 上野殿御書]） との表記がある。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}参考文献
秋山敬『棟札の基礎的研究』(2010年２月、岩田書院)
{{日本の建築}} {{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{歴史資料}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q8008834]] Wikidata name: Sign on a building's pillar stating the construction date and other info [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munafuda|qq=}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuga Residence|qq=|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and [[:en:Dwelling|houses]] to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|qq=|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to [[:en:Chūson-ji|Chūson-ji]] in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

[[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munefuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=Munefuda at {{ill|Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|qq=|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the inscription from Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munahuda, Munehuda }}
'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|qq=|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of [[:en:Hōan|Hōan era]] (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8008834]] Wikidata name: Sign on a building's pillar stating the construction date and other info [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munafuda|qq=}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuga Residence|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and [[:en:Dwelling|houses]] to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to [[:en:Chūson-ji|Chūson-ji]] in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of [[:en:Hōan|Hōan era]] (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].

== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to [[:en:Chūson-ji|Chūson-ji]] in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of [[:en:Hōan|Hōan era]] (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of [[:en:Hōan|Hōan era]] (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and [[:en:Dwelling|houses]] to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to [[:en:Chūson-ji|Chūson-ji]] in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8008834]] Wikidata name: Sign on a building's pillar stating the construction date and other info [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munafuda|qq=}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuga Residence|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and [[:en:Dwelling|houses]] to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to [[:en:Chūson-ji|Chūson-ji]] in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

[[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munefuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=Munefuda at {{ill|Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the inscription from Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munahuda, Munehuda }}
'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of [[:en:Hōan|Hōan era]] (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to [[:en:Chūson-ji|Chūson-ji]] in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==

== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munahuda, Munehuda }}
'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of [[:en:Hōan|Hōan era]] (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of [[:en:Hōan|Hōan era]] (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and [[:en:Dwelling|houses]] to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to [[:en:Chūson-ji|Chūson-ji]] in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Munefuda]][[ja:棟札]][[zh:上樑記牌]]
{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munafuda|qq=}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuga Residence|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and {{ill|Dwelling|en|Dwelling|WD=Q699405|lt=houses}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=beam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|en|Chūson-ji|WD=Q2660144|lt=Chūson-ji}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

<!-- Chinese --><!-- Japanese --><!--Munafuda--><!--Munafuda--><!--Munafuda--><!--Munafuda--> ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munefuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=Munefuda at {{ill|Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the inscription from Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munahuda, Munehuda }}
'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of {{ill|Hōan|en|Hōan|WD=Q1194464|lt=Hōan era}} (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|en|Chūson-ji|WD=Q2660144|lt=Chūson-ji}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])

== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of {{ill|Hōan|en|Hōan|WD=Q1194464|lt=Hōan era}} (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|en|Chūson-ji|WD=Q2660144|lt=Chūson-ji|1=Chūson-ji}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
