A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of {{ill|Hōan|en|Hōan|WD=Q1194464|lt=Hōan era|1=Hōan}} (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].

== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).

'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and {{ill|Dwelling|en|Dwelling|WD=Q699405|lt=houses|1=Dwelling}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|en|Chūson-ji|WD=Q2660144|lt=Chūson-ji|1=Chūson-ji}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

{{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munafuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=A {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munafuda|qq=}} from the {{ill|Kuga Residence|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuga Residence|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the year Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munafuda, Munefuda }}
'''Munafuda''' (棟札, also read as ''Munehuda'') are plaques installed in elevated areas within structures like {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|寺社|1=Temples and Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|13=User:Immanuelle/Temples and Shrines|qq=}} and {{ill|Dwelling|en|Dwelling|WD=Q699405|lt=houses|1=Dwelling}} to record and commemorate their construction or renovation.
== Overview ==
Typically, a munafuda is made from wood or copper and is nailed onto {{ill|ridge beams|ja|棟木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ridge beams|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|rafters|ja|梁 (建築)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafters|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538319}}. In some cases, the information is written directly on structural components, referred to as "beam inscriptions" (梁上銘), though their purpose is essentially the same.<ref>Yorisuke Numata, "Evolution of Munafuda," in *Archaeological Journal*, Vol. 8, Issue 7 (No. 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content on a munafuda typically includes the purpose of the construction or renovation, the date, the names of the builder(s) and sponsor(s), and other construction records. Some plaques are simple, while others feature intricate details or illustrations. Since munafuda are placed in locations not visible to the general public, they are often forgotten over time. They are sometimes rediscovered during demolition or repair projects.
The information on munafuda, despite occasional inaccuracies, serves as critical historical evidence, providing insights into the history and culture of a building or region. Many munafuda have been designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munafuda dates back to {{ill|Chūson-ji|en|Chūson-ji|WD=Q2660144|lt=Chūson-ji|1=Chūson-ji}} in Iwate Prefecture, with an inscription from the third year of the Hōan era (1122).
== Munafuda in Literature ==
A reference to munafuda appears in the writings of Nichiren during the Kenji era: >"Regarding the matter of the munafuda, I have received the request and will pass it to this Lord Hōki. This inscription commemorates the construction of the Jeta Grove Monastery by the millionaire Sudatta. However, due to certain karmic causes, the millionaire faced fire disasters seven times, as he reported to the Buddha…" ([http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Ueno-dono Gosho])
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Akiyama Kei, *Basic Research on Munafuda*, Iwata Shoin, February 2010.

<!-- Chinese --><!-- Japanese --><!--Munafuda--><!--Munafuda--><!--Munafuda--><!--Munafuda--> [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]] ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox East Asian name |title=Munefuda |img=Munefuda.jpg |imgwidth=200px |caption=Munefuda at {{ill|Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|ja|長島水辺のやすらぎパーク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashima Waterside Serenity Park|1=Nagashima Mizube-no Yasuragi park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11652282}}, showing the inscription from Meiji 12 (1879).
|chinesetitle=上樑記牌 |chinese=上樑記牌 |tradchi=上樑記牌 |simpchi=上梁记牌
|japanesetitle=棟札 |kanji=棟札 |kana=むなふだ、むねふだ |hepburn=Munahuda, Munehuda }}
'''Munefuda''' (棟札, むなふだ or むねふだ) is a type of plaque affixed to the interior high places, such as ridge beams or {{ill|梁 (architecture)|ja|梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/梁 (architecture)|1=hari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15940517}} in buildings like temples, shrines, or houses. It serves as a record or commemoration of the construction or repair of the structure.
== Overview ==
Typically, munefuda are wooden plaques or copper plates nailed into place. Sometimes the inscriptions are directly written on the building material itself, referred to as "梁上銘" (Ryojōmei), but the purpose is the same.<ref>Numata Yorisuke, "The History of Munefuda," Archaeological Journal, Vol. 8, No. 7 (Issue 222), March 1918.</ref>
The content of a munefuda usually includes the purpose of the construction or repair, the date, and the names of the builders or carpenters, as well as other relevant details. These inscriptions vary greatly in style, from simple text to detailed records with drawings. Because they are placed in locations not visible to regular users, their existence is often forgotten over time. However, they are sometimes rediscovered during dismantling or repair work.
Munefuda inscriptions, though sometimes containing errors, serve as valuable historical records regarding the building and its surrounding area's history and culture. Many munefuda are designated as cultural properties. The oldest known munefuda is from Chūson-ji Temple in Iwate Prefecture, dated to the 3rd year of {{ill|Hōan|en|Hōan|WD=Q1194464|lt=Hōan era|1=Hōan}} (1122 CE).
== Munefuda in Literature ==
A reference to munefuda appears in the first year of Kenji in the writings of Nichiren: "Regarding the munefuda, I have written it and presented it to the lord of Hōki. This scripture tells of the wealthy Sudatta building the Jeta Grove Monastery. However, due to some karmic reasons, Sudatta faced seven fires... ({{ill|Letter to Lord Ueno|ja|上野殿御書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Letter to Lord Ueno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})". For more, see [http://tidouji.la.coocan.jp/gosyo/sinnpenn/0902.pdf Letter to Lord Ueno].
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== References ==
Akiyama Takashi, "Fundamental Studies on Munefuda" (February 2010, Iwata Shoin).
